game_description: Give one-word clues to help teammates identify secret agent cards.
filter_1_label: Are you the Spymaster this round?

strategies.strategy_detailed: Before giving any clue, map all your cards into clusters by theme. Identify which cards share 2-3 strong associations. Also note which of your cards sit dangerously close in meaning to the assassin or opponent cards — avoid those clusters entirely.
strategy_stealth: Map cards into theme clusters first
Flag cards near assassin — avoid them

strategies.strategy_detailed: Your first clue should be your safest high-number clue — 2 or 3 cards with minimal risk of hitting the assassin or opponent cards. Opening with a risky 4-card clue that backfires often costs the game. Establish momentum, then take bigger swings.

Guess the most obvious match first. If wrong, stop — don't guess more. When your spymaster gives a 3-clue, pick the 2 you're most confident about, then discuss the third. Never guess all 3 without discussion unless you're certain all connections.
strategy_stealth: Stop if first guess is wrong

strategies.strategy_detailed: If one of your cards shares strong associations with the assassin, clue your other cards around it first. Then give that risky card its own isolated clue with a distinct angle the assassin doesn't share. Never bundle it with other cards in a multi-word clue.
strategy_stealth: Give it a distinct angle only

strategies.strategy_detailed: When unsure, say so aloud. Your spymaster can only confirm with facial expressions — read them carefully. A subtle reaction often signals you're on the right track. If genuinely uncertain between two cards, pick the one that doesn't connect to opponent colors at all.
strategy_stealth: Say unsure aloud — read SM's face
Pick card not near opponent colors

strategies.strategy_detailed: When opponent is one card away from winning, prioritize a clue that covers 2+ cards even if one connection is weaker than ideal. Speed beats elegance. A slightly risky 2-card clue that wins this turn is better than a perfect 1-card clue that arrives too late.
strategy_stealth: Risky connection OK to finish fast

strategies.strategy_detailed: Using a leftover guess on a card you missed from a previous clue is almost always correct. Your spymaster's prior clue still points to that card. Use the extra guess on any card you have strong confidence in — never guess randomly to use it.
strategy_stealth: Prior clue still valid — use it
Never guess randomly on extra turn

strategies.strategy_detailed: If operatives consistently miss your intended connections, simplify. Give 1-word clues that are unambiguous even if they only cover 1 card. Rebuilding trust through safe clues restores momentum better than doubling down on a complex approach that keeps failing.
strategy_stealth: Don't repeat a failing clue style

strategies.strategy_detailed: Final card clue should be hyper-specific — a clue that only connects to that exact card with no ambiguity. If you have two cards left, give a 2-clue that connects them tightly. Never give a 1-clue when 2 remain unless one is genuinely isolated.

strategies.condition: One card left, must guess correctly
strategies.strategy_detailed: Discuss as a team before guessing the final card. Enumerate which remaining cards the clue could mean. Eliminate any that connect to opponent colors. If two equally valid options remain, pick the one that's semantically closer to the clue word itself — not the most obvious surface match.
strategy_stealth: Pick semantically closest to clue word
